summary:
During embryonic development, different sets of pharyngeal arch derivatives contribute to distinct parts of the tongue; specifically, the sulcus terminalis delineates these regions into anterior vs. posterior sections during growth processes leading up to full adult structure configuration.
The anatomical distinction arises due to separate contributions from various pharyngeal arch components with a definitive line being marked at their interface via the sulcus terminalis which separates functional zones within this vital organ for taste sensation among other roles.. Agent1’s summary effectively captures how specific segments originate differently based upon early embryological stages but simplifies it further:

During fetal development, varying portions of the tongue are derived from different pharyngeal arches—specifically, the anterior part mainly develops from earlier arches whereas the back does so later involving more extensive structures. This results in a natural division indicated clinically by the *sulcus terminalis*, serving both functionally and structurally as a marker distinguishing primary sensory areas frontwise against deeper muscle groups rearwardly along its course through central lingual anatomy. 

In essence,
\[ \textbf{sulcus terminalis} \] 
marks where predominantly neural crest-derived cells forming muscles meet those creating papillae containing tastebuds etc., thus defining critical boundaries needed operationally & pathologically alike throughout life postnatally too!
